title: Mombasa: Key outcomes from the Our Ocean Conference in Kenya - Carbon Brief
gist: The Our Ocean Conference in Mombasa generated $6.4bn in pledged finance and 320 voluntary commitments, with African nations playing a leading role in driving ambition on deep-sea mining moratoriums, fisheries transparency and high-seas marine protected areas. The High Seas Treaty (effective January 2026, 90 parties) faces a tight window to establish MPAs before its first COP in January 2027. The world set a 2024 fisheries/aquaculture production record of 235 million tonnes, with aquaculture growing ~5% annually while capture fisheries stagnate and over one-third of marine stocks remain overfished.

themes.theme: High Seas Treaty — implementation race to COP1

insights.claim: The High Seas Treaty (BBNJ Agreement) entered into force on 17 January 2026 and had 90 state parties as of the Mombasa conference, with its first Conference of the Parties set for January 2027 in New York City.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: The treaty is live law — the January 2027 COP1 is a critical deadline where foundational rules for high-seas MPAs will be set, creating a short window for companies with deep-sea interests to shape outcomes.
insights.quote: The Agreement on the Conservation and Sustainable Use of Marine Biological Diversity of Areas Beyond National Jurisdiction – also known as the BBNJ Agreement or the High Seas Treaty – entered into force on 17 January 2026.

insights.claim: The director of the High Seas Alliance said achieving the '30 by 30' ocean protection target by 2030 under the High Seas Treaty is 'very, very tight' and will require governments to begin MPA proposals before COP1 in January 2027.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: A compressed timeline for high-seas MPA designation means near-term regulatory uncertainty for any deep-sea commercial activity — mining, fishing, or cable placement — in international waters.
insights.quote: It will be very, very tight. It's definitely possible, but it requires really strong government leadership and prioritisation.

insights.claim: South Africa and the EU announced plans to propose a high-seas MPA linking two sub-Antarctic protected areas at the Mombasa conference, while Senegal, Mauritania, the Gambia and Guinea-Bissau committed to creating at least two transboundary West African MPAs.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Concrete MPA proposals from developed and developing country coalitions are the key variable — these will define which ocean areas face new access restrictions post-COP1.
insights.quote: representatives from South Africa and the EU detailed plans to propose a high-seas MPA that would link two existing protected areas in the sub-Antarctic

themes.theme: Marine protected areas — designation vs. implementation gap

insights.claim: More than one-third of all existing marine protected areas globally — covering over 10 million square kilometres — originated from commitments made at previous Our Ocean Conference editions.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: The OOC has outsized influence on global marine governance — its voluntary commitment mechanism has demonstrably shaped which ocean areas are off-limits to exploitation.
insights.quote: more than one-third of the Earth's MPAs stemmed from announcements made at the OOC – a total area of more than 10m square kilometres (km2).

insights.claim: At least half of existing marine protected areas worldwide remain unimplemented or operationally ineffective, and only 3.5% of the global ocean is 'fully and highly' protected, according to a Smithsonian Tropical Research Institute report.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: The implementation gap means paper MPA designations significantly overstate actual ocean protection — enforcement and funding are the critical missing variables.
insights.quote: at least half of existing MPAs remain unimplemented or operationally ineffective, while just 3.5% of the global ocean is 'fully and highly' protected.

insights.claim: Two new Chilean MPAs in the Juan Fernandez region (337,000 km2) and the Azores Marine Park (287,000 km2) — the largest network of protected areas in the North Atlantic — were designated at or around the Mombasa conference.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Large new MPA designations in the Atlantic and Pacific materially affect fishing access rights and future bioprospecting opportunities in affected areas.
insights.quote: the establishment this year of two new MPAs in the Juan Fernández region of Chile, protecting a total of around 337,000km2 of ocean, and the approval of the Azores Marine Park, which will span 287,000km2

insights.claim: If all pledged MPAs from Our Ocean Conference editions were fully implemented, global ocean protection would cover approximately 14.4 million km2, or 4% of the ocean.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Even full OOC pledge implementation would leave global ocean protection far short of the 30% '30 by 30' target — indicating massive regulatory and financial gaps remain.
insights.quote: If all pledged MPAs were to be implemented, it would represent protection for around 14.4m km2 or 4% of the ocean.

insights.claim: Malawi became the first African country and 41st country overall to support a precautionary pause on deep-sea mining, followed by Kenya and Madagascar at the Mombasa conference.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Growing African support for a deep-sea mining pause increases the political weight behind a moratorium and raises regulatory risk for companies with deep-sea mineral exploration plans.
insights.quote: Malawi became the first African country to support such a pause – and the 41st country overall to support a precautionary pause or moratorium on the activity.

insights.claim: A University of British Columbia professor told the Mombasa conference that the key question on deep-sea mining is not whether minerals exist but whether extraction delivers net-positive impact — requiring a broad cost-benefit analysis.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: The cost-benefit framing, rather than outright prohibition, signals that scientific evidence thresholds will be central to any eventual regulatory regime — companies must prepare rigorous environmental impact cases.
insights.quote: It's not whether they have the minerals, it's whether extracting them gives a net-positive impact.

themes.theme: Fisheries — record production masks structural fragility

insights.claim: Global fisheries and aquaculture set a new 2024 production record of 235 million tonnes (92m tonnes from capture fisheries, 103m tonnes from farmed fish and 40m tonnes of algae), according to the UN FAO's biannual state of world fisheries report.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Record production driven almost entirely by aquaculture growth — capture fisheries are stagnant — signals that future seafood supply will depend on aquaculture investment, particularly in Asia.
insights.quote: the world set a new record for fisheries and aquaculture in 2024 – producing a total of 235m tonnes of fish and algae.

insights.claim: Global aquaculture production has grown at an average annual rate of just under 5% since 2000, with 89% of production occurring in Asia, while capture fisheries output has been largely flat.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Asian aquaculture dominance is structural — investors in seafood supply chains face concentration risk in Asia and a compelling case for aquaculture over wild-catch exposure.
insights.quote: aquaculture production has increased by an average annual percentage rate of just under 5%...the vast majority of aquaculture production – 89% – occurs in Asia.

insights.claim: More than one-third of the world's marine fish stocks are currently overfished, with significant regional and species variation, and climate change is expected to play an increasing role in driving fisheries unsustainability.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Wild-catch supply constraints will tighten — a tailwind for aquaculture and alternative protein companies, and a risk to food security in developing markets dependent on marine protein.
insights.quote: more than one-third of the world's marine fish stocks are overfished, with significant variation based on region and species

insights.claim: The Mombasa Declaration on fisheries transparency was signed by 16 governments (8 African) committing to anti-IUU fishing principles, while the EU committed EUR 46m through Horizon Europe to fisheries research.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Fisheries transparency commitments combined with EU research funding signal a regulatory tightening trajectory — companies reliant on opaque fishing operations in West African waters face rising compliance costs.
insights.quote: The declaration, which was signed by 16 national governments – eight of them from Africa – commits parties to follow a set of principles laid out in the Global Charter for Fisheries Transparency.

themes.theme: Coral reef protection as climate resilience priority

insights.claim: A new study presented at Mombasa mapped over 165,000 km2 of coral reefs across 70 countries identified as most likely to survive climate change, building on a 2018 project that identified an optimal portfolio of 50 climate-resilient reefs.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Climate-resilient reef mapping creates a scientifically defensible basis for prioritising conservation spending — a key tool for development banks and sovereigns allocating nature-based finance.
insights.quote: the new work mapped more than 165,000km2 of coral reefs across 70 countries...found to have the best chances of persisting in the face of climate change

insights.claim: Norway pledged NOK 20m ($2m) to the Global Fund for Coral Reefs at the Mombasa conference, while four more countries (Comoros, Dominican Republic, Kenya, UK) joined the high-level climate-resilient coral reef protection commitment — with a target of 31 signatories by COP31.
insights.speaker: Giuliana Viglione
insights.so_what: Coral reef finance remains dramatically underfunded relative to scale of need — the small pledges signal an early-stage market with significant scaling potential for climate-adaptation finance instruments.
insights.quote: Norway pledged to allocate NOK 20m ($2m) to the Global Fund for Coral Reefs
